err: symbol. ISO 639-3 language code for Erre.
fey: adjective. About to die; doomed; on the verge of sudden or violent death.

froe: noun. A cleaving tool for splitting cask staves and shingles from a block of wood.
from: preposition. Used to indicate source or provenance.

fryer: noun. A machine or container for frying food.

lore: noun. All the facts and traditions about a particular subject that have been accumulated over time through education or experience.
lorry: noun. A large and heavy motor vehicle designed to carry goods or soldiers; a truck
lory: noun. Any of various small, brightly coloured parrots native to Australasia.
lye: noun. An alkaline liquid made by leaching ashes (usually wood ashes).

mel: symbol. ISO 639-3 language code for Central Melanau.
merry: adjective. Jolly and full of high spirits; happy.
mole: noun. A naevus, a pigmented, slightly raised, and sometimes hairy spot on the skin.
moly: noun. A magic herb or plant used by Odysseus to overcome Circe.
ole: symbol. ISO 639-3 language code for Olekha.
omer: noun. A former small Hebrew unit of dry volume equal to about 2.3 L or 2.1 quarts.
ore: symbol. ISO 639-3 language code for Orejón.
orle: noun. A bordure that runs around the outline of a shield without touching the edge.
ormer: noun. An abalone or sea-ear, particularly Haliotis tuberculata, common in the Channel Islands.
oyer: noun. A hearing in a civil case which is based on the content of a document, in which the plaintiff is required to produce the document.
reform: noun. The change of something that is defective, broken, inefficient or otherwise negative, in order to correct or improve it
rye: noun. A grain used extensively in Europe for making bread, beer, and (now generally) for animal fodder.

yom: symbol. ISO 639-3 language code for Yombe.
